title: Iowa vs Florida — Round of 32 Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • Round of 32 • Tampa, FL
venue: Benchmark International Arena, Tampa, Florida • TBS
extraInfo: Winner advances to the Sweet 16 in the South Region

stakes.callout: The defending national champions against the most unusual coaching journey in America. Florida won the title last April and just destroyed Prairie View A&M by 59 points — the second-largest margin in tournament history. Iowa's coach went 395-91 with four DII national championships before reaching the Big Ten, and his star player has followed him across three programs. This is a 1-vs-9 matchup where Iowa's efficient offense (36th KenPom) runs into Florida's dominant frontcourt. The Gators are 10.5-point favorites, but Iowa hasn't trailed yet in this tournament.

cards.label: Defending Champions
cards.value: Florida won the 2025 national championship, beating Houston 65-63 after trailing by 12 in the second half. Todd Golden became the youngest champion since Jim Valvano in 1983. They earned a #1 seed for back-to-back years — a first in program history. They're trying to go back-to-back for the second time, after Joakim Noah's squad did it in 2006-07.

cards.label: Iowa's Cinderella Shot
cards.value: Iowa hasn't reached the Sweet 16 since 1999. The Hawkeyes are 1-5 all-time against #1 seeds in the tournament (lone win: Syracuse in 1980). But McCollum's teams are built to be prepared. He's 426-95 career (.818) and has won everywhere he's coached — DII, MVC, Big Ten Tournament.

cards.label: Florida's One Weakness
cards.value: The Gators rank 316th nationally in three-point shooting (31.25%). They lost their entire championship backcourt (Clayton, Martin, Richard) and rebuilt around the frontcourt. They lost to Vanderbilt 91-74 in the SEC Tournament semifinals. When they can't score inside, they struggle. Iowa's path to an upset runs through exploiting this.

cards.label: The Size Mismatch
cards.value: Florida starts Condon (6-10), Chinyelu (6-10), and has Handlogten (7-1) off the bench. Chinyelu averages a double-double (11.2 PPG, 11.5 RPG) and set the program record with 19 this season. Iowa's tallest regular is 6-9. Florida dominated Prairie View 64-10 in the paint. Can Iowa survive the interior?

styles.title: McCollum's Efficient Machine
bullets: Offense through Stirtz: Bennett Stirtz averages 19.9 PPG and 4.4 APG as an All-Big Ten, All-America honorable mention guard. Iowa's offense ranks 36th in KenPom (118.3). Everything runs through him — pick-and-roll, dribble handoffs, late-clock creation. Even on an off night against Clemson (4-of-17 FG), he led the team with 16 points and 5 assists.

Won the first round without their best shot: Iowa beat Clemson 67-61 despite Stirtz shooting 4-of-17. Kael Combs scored 15 on 5-of-7, Folgueiras had 14 with 7-of-7 free throws. McCollum's system doesn't collapse when the star struggles — others step up. That depth could matter against Florida.

Rebounding edge: Iowa outrebounded Clemson 40-27 in the first round. Manyawu (4.7 RPG, .640 FG%) and Banks (4.7 RPG) provide glass work. Against Florida's monsters on the boards, Iowa will need that same effort to have any chance.

Elite free throw shooting: Iowa shoots 77.0% from the line (29th nationally). Folgueiras went 7-of-7 against Clemson. In a game where Florida might foul aggressively inside, Iowa's ability to convert at the stripe matters.

Weakness — three-point shooting: Iowa shoots 35.5% from three (163rd nationally). Against Clemson, they went 6-of-21 from deep. If Florida's length contests Iowa's threes and the Hawkeyes can't get to the line, the offense could stall.

styles.title: The Championship Frontcourt
bullets: Dominant inside presence: Florida averages 87.6 PPG (9th nationally) with a frontcourt of Haugh (17.0 PPG), Condon (14.9 PPG, 7.5 RPG, 3.5 APG), and Chinyelu (11.2 PPG, 11.5 RPG). They outscored Prairie View 64-10 in the paint. The Gators score by overwhelming you on the glass and in the post.

Record-setting tournament opener: Florida 114, Prairie View 55. 59-point margin — second-largest in NCAA Tournament history. Shot 64.3% from the field, dished 29 assists with just 7 turnovers. Seven players scored in double figures. That's the kind of depth Iowa has to contend with.

Chinyelu is a wrecking ball: Rueben Chinyelu broke the Florida program record with 19 double-doubles this season. He also broke the single-season offensive rebounding record (137). He had 15 points and 21 rebounds against LSU. Iowa has no one who can match his physicality.

Defensive backbone: Florida ranks 30th in KenPom defensive efficiency (98.8). Chinyelu is on the Naismith Defensive Player of the Year watch list. They're a top-5 rebounding team nationally. Their size makes it hard for opponents to get clean looks inside.

Weakness — three-point shooting: Florida ranks 316th nationally at 31.25% from three. They shot 8-of-37 from deep in the SEC Tournament. When opponents pack the paint and dare them to shoot, the Gators can go cold. Klavzar (40.6% from three) is the lone reliable perimeter threat off the bench.

keys.title: Iowa must survive the paint.
keys.description: Florida outscored Prairie View 64-10 inside and has Condon (6-10), Chinyelu (6-10), and Handlogten (7-1) rotating through. Iowa's tallest regular rotation player is 6-9. If Chinyelu gets easy position and Condon uses his 3.5 APG to find cutters, Florida will score 70+ without hitting a single three. Iowa needs Manyawu (.640 FG%) to be physical and avoid foul trouble, and they need their guards to help in the post without leaving shooters open.

keys.title: Exploit Florida's 316th-ranked three-point shooting.
keys.description: Florida shoots 31.25% from three — 316th in Division I. They shot 8-of-37 from deep in the SEC Tournament. Iowa's defense (103rd KenPom) should pack the paint, force Florida's guards to beat them from outside, and dare anyone besides Klavzar (40.6% from three) to shoot. If Florida's guards go cold from deep, Iowa can hang around even if the paint battle is lopsided.

keys.title: Stirtz needs to be Stirtz.
keys.description: Against Clemson, Stirtz went 4-of-17 from the field and Iowa still won because Combs and Folgueiras picked up the slack. Against Florida, Iowa can't afford another off night from their 19.9 PPG scorer. Stirtz needs to attack the basket, get to the free throw line (he shoots 84.5%), and find Banks for open threes. If Stirtz scores 22+ efficiently, Iowa has a real chance. If Florida holds him under 15 on tough shooting again, the size advantage becomes insurmountable.

keys.title: Tempo determines the outcome.
keys.description: Florida wants to run. They average 87.6 PPG and scored 114 against Prairie View. Iowa averages 75.0 PPG and beat Clemson 67-61 in a grind. If Iowa can slow the game into the mid-60s with half-court execution and defensive stops, they negate Florida's depth and transition advantage. If Florida pushes the pace and gets out in the open court, their athletes and size overwhelm Iowa.

keys.title: Free throws and turnovers.
keys.description: Iowa shoots 77.0% from the line (29th nationally). Florida had just 7 turnovers against Prairie View and dished 29 assists. In a tight game, Iowa's free throw advantage could matter — especially if Florida fouls while trying to contest inside. But Iowa must also take care of the ball: Florida's length and athleticism generate steals (Fland had 8 in one game against Alabama). Every turnover against Florida is a fast break the other way.

prediction.summary: This is a classic 1-vs-9 where the top seed is the clear favorite, but the 9-seed has a specific path to an upset. Florida's frontcourt (Condon, Chinyelu, Haugh) is too big and too talented for Iowa to match inside. But the Gators rank 316th in three-point shooting, and Iowa's defense can pack the paint and dare Florida to beat them from the perimeter. Bennett Stirtz is the best guard on the floor at 19.9 PPG, and if he has the game he didn't have against Clemson, Iowa can keep this competitive. Florida's depth, size, and championship pedigree are the difference — but this is March, and McCollum's teams are always prepared.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: Florida establishes the interior immediately. Chinyelu and Condon combine for early baskets inside, and the Gators build a 6-8 point lead through physicality on the glass. Iowa's guards struggle to adjust to Florida's length. But Stirtz finds a couple of mid-range looks, and Banks hits an early three to keep Iowa within striking distance.

phases.title: Mid-First Half to Halftime
phases.description: Iowa settles into McCollum's half-court offense and slows the pace. Folgueiras connects as a passer from the forward spot, finding cutters against Florida's aggressive interior defense. Iowa gets to the free throw line and chips away. Florida leads by 5-7 at halftime, somewhere around 36-30, in a game that's slower than the Gators want.

phases.title: Second Half Push
phases.description: McCollum makes halftime adjustments — the man who's 426-95 always has a plan. Stirtz heats up in pick-and-roll, getting to the basket against Fland and Lee. Iowa cuts the lead to 2-3 points. But Golden counters with Klavzar off the bench, who hits a pair of threes to push the lead back to 8. Florida's depth — seven players who scored in double figures against Prairie View — wears Iowa down.

phases.title: Final 5 Minutes
phases.description: Iowa makes one last push behind Stirtz free throws and a Banks three. The lead shrinks to 4. But Chinyelu grabs an offensive rebound and converts, and Condon finds Haugh for a mid-range jumper. Florida's experience — a team that overcame a 12-point deficit to win the national championship — closes the door. The Gators advance, but Iowa makes them earn it.

projectedScore.rationale: Florida's frontcourt is too much for Iowa to handle over 40 minutes. Chinyelu's rebounding and Condon's versatility create a consistent advantage inside. Iowa's three-point shooting (35.5%) isn't good enough to keep pace from outside, and Florida's defense (30th KenPom) limits clean looks. Stirtz gets his 20, but Florida's depth and size carry the day. The 9-point margin reflects a game that's competitive for 30 minutes before the Gators pull away.

players.name: Bennett Stirtz
players.meta: G • 6-4 • Senior • All-Big Ten, AP All-America HM
players.bio:
From Liberty, Missouri. Has followed coach McCollum across three programs: Northwest Missouri State (DII), Drake, and Iowa. Was Missouri Valley Player of the Year at Drake (19.2 PPG). Named All-Big Ten and AP All-America honorable mention. Against Clemson in the first round, he scored 16 with 5 assists despite going just 4-of-17 from the field — showing he impacts the game even when his shot isn't falling. Shoots 84.5% from the free throw line. The best guard on the floor Sunday.
Read more →

players.name: Tavion Banks
players.bio: Senior wing who shoots an incredible 47.2% from three. At 6-7, he provides both floor spacing and rebounding. Against Florida's defense that dares opponents to shoot from outside, Banks is Iowa's secret weapon. If he hits 3-4 threes, Iowa's upset chances skyrocket.

players.name: Kael Combs
players.bio: From Nixa, Missouri. Third college stop after Wyoming and Drake — followed McCollum to Iowa. Was the hero of the Clemson game: 15 points on 5-of-7 shooting. A Missouri Class 6 all-state player who averaged 23.4 PPG in high school. When Stirtz had an off night, Combs kept Iowa alive. He'll need to do it again if Stirtz struggles against Florida's length.

players.name: Alvaro Folgueiras
players.bio: Junior forward from Spain who averaged 2.3 APG from the forward position — a testament to McCollum's ball-movement system. Against Clemson, scored 14 points and went 7-of-7 from the free throw line. His passing and composure in the half-court are critical for Iowa against Florida's aggressive defense.

players.name: Thomas Haugh
players.bio:
From New Oxford, Pennsylvania. Attended Perkiomen School with teammate Xaivian Lee, where they won a state championship together. Both parents competed at Shippensburg University. Grandfather played football for the Baltimore Colts. Named NABC Second-Team and USBWA Third-Team All-America in his first season as a full-time starter. Named to the Wooden Award Midseason Top 25. Scored 14 in the Prairie View blowout. Florida's leading scorer and most complete offensive player.
Read more →

players.name: Alex Condon
players.bio:
From Perth, Western Australia. Trained with the Australian national team and the NBA Global Academy. Returned to Florida after testing the 2025 NBA Draft waters. At 6-10 with 3.5 assists per game from the forward position, he's one of the most versatile players in the country. Leads the team in blocks (1.5 BPG). His ability to pass out of the post makes Florida's offense nearly impossible to guard with a traditional defense.
Read more →

players.name: Rueben Chinyelu
players.bio:
Sophomore center who averages a double-double (11.2 PPG, 11.5 RPG) and set the Florida program record with 19 double-doubles, breaking a record that had stood since 1976. Broke the single-season offensive rebounding record with 137. Had 15 points and 21 rebounds against LSU. On the Naismith Defensive Player of the Year watch list. The kind of physical presence Iowa simply doesn't have an answer for.
Read more →

players.name: Boogie Fland
players.bio: Transferred from Arkansas, where he averaged 13.5 PPG and 5.1 APG as a freshman. Led all scorers with 16 points in the Prairie View blowout. Had 8 steals in a single game against Alabama. Florida's primary ball-handler who replaced the departed Walter Clayton Jr. His ability to push tempo and create turnovers makes him dangerous in transition.

funFacts.title: Three National Championships (Florida)
funFacts.body: Florida has won three NCAA titles (2006, 2007, 2025). The 2006-07 squad under Billy Donovan became the last team to win back-to-back championships. Now the Gators are trying to do it again. No program has gone back-to-back since.

funFacts.title: Three Programs, One Bond (Iowa)
funFacts.body: Bennett Stirtz followed Ben McCollum from Northwest Missouri State to Drake to Iowa. DII to Missouri Valley POY to All-Big Ten. Three schools, one coach, and a 20-PPG scorer who keeps getting better at every level.

funFacts.title: Second-Largest Blowout in Tournament History (Florida)
funFacts.body: Florida's 114-55 win over Prairie View A&M was the second-largest margin of victory in NCAA Tournament history — only behind Loyola Chicago's 69-point win over Tennessee Tech in 1963. Seven Gators scored in double figures.

funFacts.title: Four DII National Titles (Iowa)
funFacts.body: Iowa coach Ben McCollum won four DII national championships at Northwest Missouri State (2017, 2019, 2021, 2022) and five NABC DII Coach of the Year awards — the most in history. His .818 winning percentage is fifth-best all-time among all collegiate coaches.

funFacts.title: 19 Double-Doubles (Florida)
funFacts.body: Rueben Chinyelu's 19 double-doubles this season broke a Florida record that had stood since Bob Smyth set it in 1975-76. He also broke the single-season offensive rebounding record with 137. He's only a sophomore.

funFacts.title: First-Ever Meeting
funFacts.body: Sunday's game is the first meeting in the all-time series between Iowa and Florida. Despite a combined 56 NCAA Tournament appearances between the programs, they've never faced each other.

funFacts.title: 47.2% From Three (Iowa)
funFacts.body: Tavion Banks shoots 47.2% from three. Against a Florida team that ranks 316th in three-point shooting (31.25%), Banks is the best perimeter shooter on the floor by a wide margin.

funFacts.title: Spain to the Big Ten (Iowa)
funFacts.body: Alvaro Folgueiras from Spain averages 2.3 APG from the forward position and went 7-of-7 from the free throw line against Clemson. In a system built on ball movement, the 6-9 Spaniard is the ideal McCollum connector.

funFacts.title: The Australian and the Slovenian (Florida)
funFacts.body: Alex Condon is from Perth, Australia, and trained with the Australian national team. Urban Klavzar is from Slovenia and competed in six FIBA championships. Florida's international pipeline gives them unique versatility.

funFacts.title: The Youngest Champ Since Valvano (Florida)
funFacts.body: Todd Golden won the 2025 national championship at age 39, making him the youngest title-winning coach since Jim Valvano in 1983. He then signed a $40.5 million extension. He reached 100 wins at Florida faster than Billy Donovan.

funFacts.title: 1-5 Against #1 Seeds (Iowa)
funFacts.body: Iowa is 1-5 all-time against #1 seeds in the NCAA Tournament. The lone win came in 1980 when Ronnie Lester's Hawkeyes beat Syracuse 88-77 on their way to the Final Four. That was 46 years ago.

funFacts.title: Iowa City Kid Returns (Iowa)
funFacts.body: Ben McCollum was born in Iowa City before moving to Storm Lake at age five. He attended Iowa basketball camps as a kid. Now he's the head coach. Full circle — and he's one win from the Sweet 16.

history.callout: This is the first-ever meeting between Iowa and Florida. Iowa has made 30 NCAA Tournament appearances with three Final Four trips (1956, 1980, 1987) but hasn't reached the Sweet 16 since 1999. Florida has three national championships (2006, 2007, 2025) and six Final Fours. The Gators are the defending champions and the South Region's #1 seed. The #9-seeded Hawkeyes are looking for the program's biggest upset since beating Syracuse in 1980.

timeline.text: Billy Donovan's Gators — led by Joakim Noah, Al Horford, and Corey Brewer — beat UCLA 73-57 for Florida's first national championship.
The same core returned and beat Ohio State 84-75 for back-to-back titles — the last program to repeat as NCAA champions.
Florida reached the Final Four as a #1 seed under Billy Donovan before losing to UConn.
Todd Golden's Gators won the national championship, beating Houston 65-63 after trailing by 12 in the second half. Golden became the youngest champion since Valvano.
Defending champions earn a #1 seed for back-to-back years — a program first. They open with a historic 114-55 win over Prairie View A&M.

subsections.title: Iowa's Tournament History
subsections.content:
Iowa has a rich but distant tournament history. The Hawkeyes reached the national championship game in 1956, losing to Bill Russell's undefeated San Francisco squad 83-71. In 1980, Ronnie Lester led Iowa to the Final Four under Lute Olson — beating #1 seed Syracuse 88-77 along the way. The 1987 team made a third Final Four trip.

But recent history has been lean. Iowa hasn't reached the Sweet 16 since 1999, and this is the program's first tournament appearance since 2023. Ben McCollum has brought new energy — winning the Big Ten Tournament in his first year and earning a bid with an almost entirely new roster. His overall record of 426-95 (.818) suggests Iowa is in good hands for the future, regardless of Sunday's result.

subsections.title: The 1-vs-9 Matchup
subsections.content:
Since the tournament expanded to 64 teams in 1985, #1 seeds have won about 80% of second-round games against #8/#9 seeds. The favorites win most of the time. But when they don't, it's usually because the underdog has a specific matchup advantage and a star player who goes off.

Iowa has both ingredients: Banks's 47.2% three-point shooting can exploit Florida's 316th-ranked perimeter defense, and Stirtz at 19.9 PPG is the best guard on the floor. The question is whether Iowa can survive Florida's interior dominance long enough for those advantages to matter. Chinyelu's 19 double-doubles say it won't be easy.
